1: Masters, treat your bondservants justly and fairly (to dikaion kai tēn isotēta — the just and the equal), knowing that you also have a Master in heaven — isotēs (equality, fairness) was a concept from Greek political philosophy (Thucydides, Aristotle) and Roman legal tradition. The command to treat slaves with equity was not conventional — Roman law gave masters near-total authority. The heavenly Master who holds earthly masters accountable relativizes all human authority structures.
2: Continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving — the combination of prayer (proseuche), watchfulness (grēgorountes), and thanksgiving (eucharistia) reflects the early Christian prayer tradition. Watchfulness in prayer is associated with eschatological alertness in the gospel tradition (Mark 13:33; Colossians 14:38: watch and pray that you may not enter into temptation).
3: And pray for us also, that God may open to us a door for the word, to declare the mystery of Christ, on account of which I am in prison — the open-door metaphor for missionary opportunity appears in 1 Cor 16:9 and 2 Corinthians 2:12. The mystery of Christ (mystērion tou Christou) is the Gentile-inclusion gospel that Paul was commissioned to proclaim (Colossians 1:27; Ephesians 3:3-6) and for which his imprisonment (desmois — bonds) is the consequence.
4: That I may make it clear, which is how I ought to speak — the clarity (phanerōsō, to make manifest) Paul seeks stands in contrast to the obscurity that the Colossian philosophy cultivated. Mystery religion and philosophical schools often prized esoteric knowledge accessible only to initiates; Paul's gospel is to be proclaimed openly.
5: Walk in wisdom (sophia) toward outsiders (tois exō), making the best use of the time (ton kairon exagorazomenoi) — the outsiders (hoi exō — those outside) was a standard Pauline designation for non-Christians (1 Corinthians 5:12-13; 1 Thessalonians 4:12). The time-buying (exagorazō ton kairon — redeeming the time) echoes the commercial language of purchasing time in the marketplace and reflects the urgency of the eschatological moment.
6: Let your speech always be gracious, seasoned with salt, so that you may know how you ought to answer each person — salt as a flavoring and preservative was essential in ancient Mediterranean life. As a metaphor for speech, it appears in rabbinic literature (speech should be flavored with salt-like wisdom, Abot R. Nathan 34). Salt also carried covenantal significance (Numbers 18:19; 2 Chronicles 13:5: covenant of salt). Knowing how to answer each person reflects the rhetorical ideal of the skilled orator who adapts his speech to each audience.
7: Tychicus will tell you all about my activities. He is a beloved brother and faithful minister and fellow servant in the Lord — Tychicus (from Tychos, meaning luck/fortune — a common Greek name) appears as Paul's emissary in Ephesians 6:21 and 2 Timothy 4:12, suggesting he was a regular carrier of Pauline correspondence. He is probably the letter-carrier for both Colossians and Ephesians.
8: I have sent him to you for this very purpose, that you may know how we are and that he may encourage your hearts — the letter-carrier in antiquity was not merely a postal delivery mechanism but an authoritative representative who could amplify and explain the letter's content verbally. Tychicus carries both the written letter and the authority to interpret and extend it.
9: And with him Onesimus, our faithful and beloved brother, who is one of you — Onesimus is the slave of Philemon (Phm 10-16) whom Paul is sending back to his master with the accompanying letter to Philemon. His identification as one of you (ek hymōn — from among you) identifies him as a Colossian. The two letters — Colossians and Philemon — are companion pieces sent with the same delegation.
10: Aristarchus my fellow prisoner greets you, and Mark the cousin of Barnabas — Aristarchus the Macedonian (from Thessalonica, Acts 20:4; Colossians 27:2) is Paul's companion from the third missionary journey. The cousin (anepsios) relationship between Barnabas and Mark (John Mark of Acts 12:12) establishes the Jerusalem connection. The split between Paul and Barnabas over Mark (Acts 15:37-39) has apparently been healed by the time of Colossians.
11: Jesus who is called Justus — this otherwise unattested believer with the Jewish name Jesus (Yeshua) and the Latin cognomen Justus (the Just) represents the Jewish-Christian tradition. His Jewish-Roman dual naming was common in the diaspora. These three are the only Jews among my fellow workers for the kingdom of God — underscoring that Paul's co-workers at the time were predominantly Gentile.
12: Epaphras, who is one of you, a servant of Christ Jesus, greets you, always struggling on your behalf in his prayers — Epaphras (Colossians 1:7) is the Colossian-born founder of the congregation. His prayer struggle (agōnizomai — competing in an athletic contest) for the Colossians mirrors Paul's own apostolic struggle (Colossians 1:29). The word is used of Gethsemane (Luke 22:44: agōnia — wrestling) and athletic competition (1 Corinthians 9:25).
13: For I bear him witness that he has worked hard for you and for those in Laodicea and in Hierapolis — the three Lycus Valley cities form Epaphras's pastoral field. Hierapolis (modern Pamukkale) was known for its spectacular travertine terraces from hot springs and its healing shrines; it had a significant Jewish community attested in inscriptions. Paul's apostolic testimony (martyreō) validates Epaphras's work.
14: Luke the beloved physician (ho iatros ho agapētos) greets you — Luke is identified as a physician only here in the NT. His medical profession is consistent with the technical precision of some medical descriptions in Luke-Acts (the woman with a hemorrhage, Luke 8:43-44; the ear of the high priest's servant, Luke 22:51). Demas greets you — Demas later abandoned Paul (2 Timothy 4:10: Demas, in love with this present world, has deserted me).
15: Give my greetings to the brothers at Laodicea, and to Nympha and the church in her house — the house church (ekklēsia kat' oikon — church according to the house) was the standard unit of early Christian community. Nympha is a woman hosting a house church in Laodicea — one of several women who hosted early Christian communities (Lydia, Acts 16:15; Priscilla, Romans 16:5; Mary, Acts 12:12; Chloe, 1 Corinthians 1:11).
16: And when this letter has been read among you, have it also read in the church of the Laodiceans — the inter-church reading of Paul's letters establishes the foundation for the eventual collection and canonical formation of the Pauline corpus. The Laodiceans letter (hē ek Laodiceias — the one from Laodicea) is now lost, unless it is the canonical Ephesians (which has no addressee in some manuscripts).
17: And say to Archippus, see that you fulfill the ministry that you have received in the Lord — Archippus is addressed in Phm 2 as Paul's fellow soldier and is apparently a leader in the Colossian/Laodicean community (possibly Philemon's son or a house-church leader). The public charge to fulfill his ministry (diakonia) adds an element of congregational accountability.
18: I, Paul, write this greeting with my own hand. Remember my chains. Grace be with you — the autograph (the closing greeting in Paul's own handwriting — Galatians 6:11; 1 Corinthians 16:21; 2 Thessalonians 3:17; Phm 19) serves as authentication: the body of the letter was dictated to an amanuensis (secretary), but the closing in Paul's hand vouches for the letter's genuineness. Remember my chains (mnēmoneuete mou tōn desmōn) is a poignant last word from the imprisoned apostle — a request for prayer and solidarity.
